title: DELEGATION and SUPERVISION in a SKILLED NURSING FACILITY (Long-Term Care): A Day-Shift Charge RN Applies the FIVE RIGHTS OF DELEGATION and the RN-vs-LVN/LPN-vs-UAP/CNA SCOPE Matrix Across a Resident Assignment (1 LVN + 2 CNAs) When a NEW ADMISSION Arrives From the Hospital AND a Previously Stable Resident ACUTELY DETERIORATES (New Fever, Confusion, Falling SpO2, and a CNA Report of Poor Intake + Coughing With Thin Liquids) — Deciding What Can and CANNOT Be Delegated (NEVER Assessment, the Initial Teaching, Evaluation, Nursing Judgment, or the Care of an UNSTABLE Resident), Understanding That Delegating a TASK Does NOT Transfer ACCOUNTABILITY, Matching RIGHT TASK / RIGHT CIRCUMSTANCE / RIGHT PERSON / RIGHT DIRECTION-COMMUNICATION / RIGHT SUPERVISION, Acting on a UAP's Abnormal Report, and Following Up to Supervise and Evaluate Outcomes
subtitle: Safe DELEGATION is a core RN leadership competency and a heavily tested NCLEX topic. This case puts T. Nguyen, RN, in charge of a long-term-care/skilled-nursing unit on day shift with one LVN/LPN and two CNAs (UAPs) and ~20 residents, when two demands collide: a NEW ADMISSION is arriving from the hospital (post-op hip, stable) and needs an admission assessment, medication reconciliation, and initial teaching, AND a previously stable resident (Mr. Ellison) ACUTELY DETERIORATES with a new fever, new confusion, a falling SpO2, and a CNA report that he ate less than 25% of his meals for two days and now COUGHS with thin liquids (a dysphagia/aspiration red flag). The RN must apply the FIVE RIGHTS OF DELEGATION — RIGHT TASK (a routine, standardized task with a predictable outcome), RIGHT CIRCUMSTANCE (a STABLE resident), RIGHT PERSON (matched to verified competence and scope), RIGHT DIRECTION/COMMUNICATION (clear, specific instructions including what and when to report back and the expected outcome, with confirmed understanding), and RIGHT SUPERVISION/EVALUATION (monitoring, follow-up, and evaluating the outcome) — and the RN/LVN/UAP SCOPE MATRIX: a UAP/CNA performs ADLs, hygiene, feeding (only when there is NO dysphagia), ambulation, repositioning, routine vital signs and weights on STABLE residents, intake/output, and point-of-care glucose per policy — collecting DATA to REPORT to the nurse, but NEVER assessing, interpreting, teaching, or (in most settings) administering medications; an LVN/LPN may administer most PO/IM/SQ medications, reinforce teaching, perform routine ostomy/wound/tube-feeding care, gather data, and monitor STABLE residents with predictable outcomes, all under the RN's direction; and the RN RETAINS the initial and ongoing ASSESSMENT, care PLANNING, the INITIAL teaching, EVALUATION of outcomes, clinical JUDGMENT, IV push/titration and the care of UNSTABLE residents. Two principles anchor the case: (1) delegating a TASK does NOT transfer ACCOUNTABILITY — the RN remains accountable for the delegation decision and for supervising it; and (2) a task that is normally delegable becomes NON-delegable when the resident is UNSTABLE or the outcome is UNPREDICTABLE (so the deteriorating resident's vital signs and assessment must be done by the RN, not a CNA). The RN therefore assesses Mr. Ellison personally, acts on the dysphagia report (hold PO, position upright, oxygen as needed, notify provider, arrange swallow evaluation), performs the new admission's RN assessment/med reconciliation/initial teaching, delegates the right STABLE-resident tasks to the LVN (scheduled meds, reinforce established teaching) and the CNAs (ADLs, routine vitals, I&O, glucose) with clear direction, and SUPERVISES and FOLLOWS UP on every delegated task — never dismissing the CNA's abnormal report, never assigning out-of-scope work (e.g., an LVN pushing/titrating an IV medication on an unstable resident or a CNA performing an admission assessment or initial teaching), and never assuming accountability ended when the task was handed off. This is DISTINCT from the acute-care charge-RN shift-assignment/triage case ([[case-078]]) and the various rapid-response/code leadership cases ([[case-079]], [[case-091]], [[case-150]], [[case-131]]): the focus here is the Five Rights and the long-term-care RN/LVN/UAP scope matrix. Step-6 evaluates whether each action is APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

patient.name: T. Nguyen, RN (Charge Nurse — not the resident)
patient.admitDate: 0700 day shift on a skilled-nursing/long-term-care unit; received handoff from night shift; responsible for ~20 residents with 1 LVN/LPN + 2 CNAs (UAPs); a new admission from the hospital is arriving this hour and a stable resident has begun to deteriorate.
patient.room: Skilled Nursing Facility, North Wing; Charge Nurse Station; team = RN (self) + 1 LVN/LPN + 2 CNAs

vitals.time: Resident A — long-term resident, stable, routine morning care due
vitals.spo2: 97% RA; T 36.8°C; alert at baseline, no dysphagia; needs bath, ambulation, morning vital signs, I&O, and a point-of-care glucose per policy — appropriate to DELEGATE to a CNA with clear direction

vitals.time: Resident B — NEW ADMISSION arriving from hospital (post-op Day 3 R hip ORIF)
vitals.spo2: 96% RA; T 37.0°C; stable but requires the RN ADMISSION ASSESSMENT, medication reconciliation (incl. enoxaparin), skin/fall-risk evaluation, and INITIAL teaching — RN responsibilities that are NOT delegable; CNA may assist with ADLs/vitals once stable

vitals.time: Resident C (Mr. Ellison) — previously stable, now ACUTELY DETERIORATING
vitals.spo2: 92% RA; T 38.6°C; NEW confusion (not at baseline); CNA reports he ate <25% of meals × 2 days and now COUGHS with thin liquids — an UNSTABLE resident: vital signs + assessment must be done BY THE RN, not delegated; possible aspiration/pneumonia or UTI/delirium

vitals.time: Resident D — stable, scheduled medications due
vitals.spo2: 98% RA; T 36.9°C; stable with an established regimen — appropriate for the LVN/LPN to administer scheduled PO/SQ meds and reinforce established teaching under the RN's direction

vitals.time: Resident E — stable, established daily dressing change
vitals.spo2: 97% RA; T 37.1°C; a healing pressure injury with an ESTABLISHED, routine dressing order (predictable, stable) may be delegated to the LVN; a NEW wound or change would require the RN's assessment first; a CNA cannot perform wound care

vitals.time: Team/assignment snapshot — re-prioritizing after the deterioration + admission
vitals.spo2: Team = RN + 1 LVN/LPN + 2 CNAs. RN re-prioritizes: personally assess the unstable resident first, act on the aspiration risk, perform the admission, and delegate stable-resident tasks with clear direction and supervision — accountability for the delegation stays with the RN

labs.v: Delegation = transferring authority to perform a selected TASK to a competent person, while the RN retains ACCOUNTABILITY. The five rights: (1) RIGHT TASK — a routine, standardized task with a predictable outcome and minimal judgment; (2) RIGHT CIRCUMSTANCE — a STABLE resident with a predictable course (an unstable/unpredictable situation is NOT delegable); (3) RIGHT PERSON — matched to the delegatee's legal scope AND verified competence; (4) RIGHT DIRECTION/COMMUNICATION — clear, specific instructions (what to do, how, by when, exactly WHAT and WHEN to report back, and the expected outcome), with confirmed understanding; (5) RIGHT SUPERVISION/EVALUATION — the RN monitors, is available, follows up, intervenes as needed, and EVALUATES the outcome.
labs.r: Right TASK (routine, predictable) + Right CIRCUMSTANCE (stable resident) + Right PERSON (scope + competence) + Right DIRECTION (clear, specific, with report-back parameters + confirmed understanding) + Right SUPERVISION/EVALUATION (monitor, follow up, evaluate).

labs.n: What the RN can NEVER delegate + accountability is NOT transferred
labs.v: The RN may NOT delegate the steps of the nursing process that require professional JUDGMENT: the initial and ongoing ASSESSMENT, NURSING DIAGNOSIS/care PLANNING, the INITIAL patient TEACHING, and the EVALUATION of outcomes — nor the care of an UNSTABLE or unpredictable resident. (An LVN/LPN may REINFORCE teaching the RN began, but the initial teaching and its evaluation remain the RN's.) A useful memory aid is that you cannot delegate what requires assessment, teaching, evaluation, or judgment. Critically, delegating a TASK does NOT transfer ACCOUNTABILITY: the RN remains accountable for the DECISION to delegate, for delegating within the delegatee's scope/competence, and for supervising and evaluating the result. The delegatee is responsible for performing the task correctly and reporting back.
labs.r: Never delegate ASSESSMENT, care PLANNING, INITIAL teaching, EVALUATION, clinical JUDGMENT, or the UNSTABLE resident. Delegating a task does NOT transfer accountability — the RN stays accountable for the delegation + supervision; the delegatee is responsible for correct performance + reporting.

labs.n: SCOPE MATRIX — RN vs LVN/LPN vs UAP/CNA
labs.v: UAP/CNA (unlicensed): ADLs, bathing/hygiene, FEEDING residents who have NO dysphagia/aspiration risk, ambulation/transfers, repositioning, routine VITAL SIGNS and weights on STABLE residents, intake/output, specimen collection, and point-of-care glucose per facility policy — they COLLECT DATA and REPORT to the nurse but do NOT assess, interpret, teach, or (in most settings) administer medications. LVN/LPN: administers most PO/IM/SQ medications, REINFORCES teaching, performs routine/established wound, ostomy, and tube-feeding care, gathers data, and monitors STABLE residents with predictable outcomes — all under the RN's direction; LVNs generally do NOT do IV-push medications, blood administration, titration, the initial admission assessment, or the care of unstable residents. RN: initial + ongoing ASSESSMENT, care PLANNING, INITIAL teaching, EVALUATION, clinical judgment, IV push/blood/titration, and the care of UNSTABLE residents.
labs.r: UAP/CNA: ADLs, hygiene, feeding (no dysphagia), ambulation, repositioning, routine vitals/weights/I&O/glucose on STABLE residents — data collection + reporting only. LVN/LPN: PO/IM/SQ meds, reinforce teaching, routine wound/ostomy/tube care, monitor STABLE residents under RN. RN: assessment, planning, initial teaching, evaluation, judgment, IV push/blood/titration, UNSTABLE residents.

labs.n: When a normally delegable task becomes NON-delegable (Right Circumstance/Right Person)
labs.v: Stability and predictability decide. A task that is routinely delegated to a CNA (e.g., taking vital signs) becomes NON-delegable when the resident is UNSTABLE or the result will require assessment/judgment to interpret — so the DETERIORATING resident's vital signs and assessment are done BY THE RN. The RN also matches the task to the delegatee's VERIFIED competence (not just the job title) and the resident's needs, then gives Right Direction: state the specific task, how and when to do it, the data to report and the parameters that require IMMEDIATE notification (e.g., 'tell me right away if his temperature is over 38, his oxygen is under 94%, or he coughs while eating/drinking'), and the expected outcome — and confirm the delegatee understands.
labs.r: Delegability depends on STABILITY + predictability: an unstable resident's vitals/assessment = RN, even if vitals are 'normally' a CNA task. Match to verified competence (not title alone); give specific direction with report-back parameters + immediate-notification thresholds + expected outcome; confirm understanding.

labs.n: SUPERVISION, follow-up, acting on a UAP report + PERTINENT NEGATIVES
labs.v: After delegating, the RN SUPERVISES — remains available, checks in, follows up that tasks were done correctly and on time, intervenes if needed, and EVALUATES the outcome and the resident's response. A UAP's report of an abnormal finding (here, poor intake + coughing with thin liquids, plus new confusion) is DATA the RN must act on: hold oral intake, keep the resident upright, provide oxygen as needed, ASSESS, notify the provider, and arrange a swallow evaluation — never dismiss it. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT delegate the unstable resident's assessment or vital-sign interpretation to a CNA; did NOT ask a CNA to perform the new admission's assessment or initial teaching; did NOT direct the LVN to push/titrate an IV medication on an unstable resident; did NOT feed or allow PO intake for a resident coughing with liquids; did NOT ignore the CNA's report because the resident 'looked fine'; did NOT assume accountability ended when the task was handed off; and did NOT fail to follow up/supervise.
labs.r: Supervise: stay available, follow up, evaluate outcomes + resident response. A UAP's abnormal report = act on it (hold PO, upright, O2, ASSESS, notify, swallow eval) — never dismiss. NEGATIVES: don't delegate unstable-resident assessment, admission assessment/initial teaching, or out-of-scope tasks (LVN IV push/titration on unstable); don't ignore UAP reports; don't assume accountability transferred; always follow up.

labs.n: Resident C (Mr. Ellison) — RN focused assessment of the deterioration
labs.v: T 38.6°C, HR 104, RR 24, SpO2 92% on room air, NEW confusion from baseline; lungs with possible crackles at the right base; coughs with thin liquids — concerning for ASPIRATION/pneumonia, with UTI/delirium also on the differential in an older adult. Unstable + judgment-dependent → the RN performs and interprets this assessment.
labs.r: Stable baseline; afebrile; SpO2 ≥ 94%; at-baseline cognition; no cough with intake. New fever + confusion + hypoxia + cough with liquids = unstable, RN assessment required.

labs.n: CNA report — oral intake + swallowing observation (DATA to escalate)
labs.v: CNA reports Mr. Ellison ate <25% of his last several meals and now coughs/chokes when drinking thin liquids. This is appropriate UAP data collection + reporting; the RN must ACT: hold PO, keep upright, notify provider, and arrange a swallow/dysphagia evaluation before further oral intake.
labs.r: Eats ≥ most of meals; no coughing/choking with food or liquids. Poor intake + coughing with thin liquids = aspiration red flag → RN action.

labs.v: Post-op Day 3 right hip ORIF, stable vital signs, on enoxaparin and scheduled analgesia; arrives with a transfer summary requiring RN admission assessment, medication reconciliation, skin and fall-risk evaluation, and initial teaching.
labs.r: Admission assessment + med reconciliation + initial teaching completed by the RN; ADLs delegable to a CNA once stable.

labs.n: Resident A — point-of-care blood glucose (CNA per policy)
labs.v: 138 mg/dL (within the resident's target range; a routine, predictable check appropriately delegated to the CNA per facility policy, with results reported to the nurse)

Stable resident with an established PO/SQ regimen and predictable response — appropriate for the LVN/LPN to administer and to reinforce established teaching under the RN's direction; no IV push or titration involved
labs.r: Stable, established regimen suitable for LVN administration under RN supervision

nursesNote: 0715 — Skilled-nursing unit, day shift; RN charge with 1 LVN/LPN + 2 CNAs and ~20 residents. Two competing demands this hour: a new admission (post-op Day 3 R hip ORIF, stable) is arriving and needs the RN admission assessment, med reconciliation, and initial teaching; and Mr. Ellison (Resident C), previously stable, has deteriorated — T 38.6, HR 104, RR 24, SpO2 92% RA, NEW confusion, and a CNA report that he ate <25% of meals × 2 days and now coughs with thin liquids (aspiration red flag). Recognized Mr. Ellison as UNSTABLE: went to him and performed the focused assessment myself (did NOT delegate the assessment or vital-sign interpretation to a CNA). Acted on the dysphagia report — held PO intake, kept him upright, applied oxygen, notified the provider, and requested a swallow evaluation and workup (possible aspiration pneumonia vs UTI/delirium). Applied the Five Rights of Delegation: delegated stable-resident ADLs, ambulation, routine vitals, I&O, and point-of-care glucose to the CNAs with clear direction and explicit report-back parameters (notify me immediately for T >38, SpO2 <94%, or coughing with intake); assigned the LVN/LPN to administer stable residents' scheduled PO/SQ meds, reinforce established teaching, and perform Resident E's established routine dressing under my supervision (a NEW wound or change would require my assessment first). Completed the new admission's RN assessment, medication reconciliation, skin/fall-risk evaluation, and initial teaching myself. Will supervise and follow up on all delegated tasks and evaluate outcomes — accountability for the delegation decisions remains mine. Did NOT delegate the unstable resident or the admission assessment/initial teaching to unlicensed staff, did NOT direct the LVN to push/titrate IV medication on an unstable resident, did NOT allow PO intake while he was coughing with liquids, did NOT dismiss the CNA's report, and did NOT assume accountability ended at hand-off.
providerOrders: DELEGATION FRAMEWORK: charge RN to apply the Five Rights of Delegation (right task, right circumstance, right person, right direction/communication, right supervision/evaluation) and the RN/LVN/UAP scope matrix across the assignment
UNSTABLE RESIDENT (Mr. Ellison): RN to perform and interpret the focused assessment (not delegable); obtain vital signs, oxygen saturation, lung sounds, and mental-status exam personally

ASPIRATION/DYSPHAGIA PRECAUTIONS: hold oral intake, keep upright, provide oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥ 94%, notify provider, and arrange a swallow/dysphagia evaluation before further PO intake; obtain ordered workup (e.g., chest imaging, urinalysis/cultures, labs) for the new fever + confusion

NEW ADMISSION (post-op hip): RN to complete the admission assessment, medication reconciliation (including enoxaparin), skin and fall-risk evaluation, and initial teaching; delegate stable ADLs to a CNA once stable

DELEGATE TO CNAs/UAPs: ADLs, hygiene, ambulation, repositioning, routine vital signs and weights on STABLE residents, intake/output, and point-of-care glucose per policy — with clear direction, report-back parameters, and immediate-notification thresholds (data collection only; no assessment/teaching/medication administration)

ASSIGN TO LVN/LPN: administer stable residents' scheduled PO/IM/SQ medications, reinforce established teaching, and perform routine/established wound, ostomy, and tube-feeding care for STABLE residents under RN direction (no IV push, titration, blood administration, admission assessment, or unstable-resident care)
SUPERVISION/EVALUATION: RN to monitor, remain available, follow up on all delegated tasks, evaluate outcomes and resident responses, and re-prioritize the assignment as conditions change
ESCALATION OF UAP REPORTS: any UAP-reported abnormal finding (poor intake, coughing with intake, new symptoms, out-of-range vitals) is to be reported to and assessed by the RN promptly

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What requires the charge RN's attention?
steps.inst: Select ALL clinically and managerially significant cues.

opts.text: Mr. Ellison (Resident C) has a new fever, new confusion, a falling SpO2 (92%), and tachypnea — a previously stable resident who is now UNSTABLE
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — an acutely deteriorating, unstable resident requires RN assessment and clinical judgment and cannot be delegated.

opts.text: The CNA reports he ate <25% of meals × 2 days and now coughs with thin liquids — a dysphagia/aspiration red flag that must be assessed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — this UAP-collected data signals aspiration risk and must be acted on by the RN, not dismissed.

opts.text: A new admission is arriving from the hospital and needs an admission assessment, medication reconciliation, and initial teaching
opts.rat: RELEVANT — admission assessment and initial teaching are RN responsibilities and must be planned into the workload.

opts.text: Routine tasks remain due for stable residents — morning vital signs, ADLs, ambulation, point-of-care glucose, and scheduled established medications
opts.rat: RELEVANT — these predictable, standardized tasks for stable residents are candidates for appropriate delegation.

opts.text: The RN has one LVN/LPN and two CNAs to coordinate across the assignment
opts.rat: RELEVANT — knowing the team's scope and competence is the basis for matching tasks to the right person.

opts.text: Because the unit is busy, the CNA can complete the new admission's assessment and teaching to save time
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Admission assessment and initial teaching require nursing judgment and are RN responsibilities — never delegated to a UAP.

opts.text: The deteriorating resident's vital signs can be delegated to the CNA and interpreted later
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. On an unstable resident, vital signs and assessment are done by the RN — the circumstance is no longer right for delegation.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What do these facts mean for delegation?

opts.text: The unstable, deteriorating resident requires RN assessment and clinical judgment — this cannot be delegated
opts.rat: CORRECT — assessment and care of an unstable resident are RN-only responsibilities.

opts.text: The CNA's report of poor intake and coughing with liquids is data the RN must act on — hold PO, position upright, assess, notify the provider, and arrange a swallow evaluation
opts.rat: CORRECT — UAPs collect and report data; acting on it (and the assessment) is the RN's role.

opts.text: The admission assessment and initial teaching are RN responsibilities; the CNA may assist with ADLs and routine vitals once the resident is stable
opts.rat: CORRECT — initial assessment/teaching stay with the RN, while stable ADLs/vitals are delegable.

opts.text: Stable residents' ADLs, hygiene, ambulation, routine vitals, I&O, and point-of-care glucose are appropriate to delegate to the CNAs
opts.rat: CORRECT — these are routine, predictable tasks within UAP scope for stable residents.

opts.text: The LVN/LPN may administer stable residents' scheduled PO/SQ medications and reinforce established teaching under the RN's direction
opts.rat: CORRECT — within LVN scope for stable residents with predictable outcomes, under RN supervision.

opts.text: Delegating these tasks transfers the RN's accountability for them to the LVN and CNAs
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Delegating a task does not transfer accountability — the RN remains accountable for the delegation and its supervision.

opts.text: Because the CNA already took his vital signs, no further nursing assessment of the deteriorating resident is needed
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Vital-sign numbers are data; the RN must still assess and interpret the unstable resident's full picture.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these actions from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Personally assess the deteriorating, unstable resident (Mr. Ellison) — airway/breathing, oxygenation, mental status, and vital signs
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The unstable resident needs immediate RN assessment and judgment.

opts.text: Act on the aspiration risk — hold PO intake, keep him upright, provide oxygen, notify the provider, and arrange a swallow evaluation
opts.rat: #2 — Protect the airway and start the workup once the deterioration is recognized.

opts.text: Complete the new admission's RN assessment, medication reconciliation, and initial teaching
opts.rat: #3 — A required RN responsibility for the incoming resident, performed after the unstable resident is addressed.

opts.text: Delegate stable residents' ADLs, routine vitals, and glucose checks to the CNAs and scheduled meds to the LVN, with clear direction
opts.rat: #4 — Distribute routine, predictable work to the right team members so care continues.

opts.text: Follow up on and supervise all delegated tasks and evaluate the outcomes
opts.rat: #5 — Supervision and evaluation complete the delegation process and remain the RN's accountability.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED.

opts.text: Assess Mr. Ellison personally; do not delegate the unstable resident's assessment
opts.rat: Assessment and care of an unstable resident are RN-only.

opts.text: Hold his oral intake, keep him upright, provide oxygen, notify the provider, and arrange a swallow evaluation after the coughing-with-liquids report
opts.rat: Protects the airway and initiates appropriate workup for aspiration risk.

opts.text: Delegate stable residents' bathing, ambulation, routine vitals, I&O, and glucose checks to the CNAs with clear direction and report-back parameters
opts.rat: Routine, predictable tasks within UAP scope, delegated with the right direction.

opts.text: Assign the LVN/LPN to administer stable residents' scheduled PO/SQ meds, reinforce established teaching, and do Resident E's established routine dressing under RN supervision
opts.rat: Within LVN scope for stable residents with predictable outcomes, under RN direction.

opts.text: Perform the new admission's RN assessment, medication reconciliation, and initial teaching yourself, then delegate stable follow-up ADLs to a CNA
opts.rat: Initial assessment and teaching are RN responsibilities; routine ADLs are delegable once stable.

opts.text: Ask a CNA to perform the new admission's assessment and initial teaching because the unit is short-staffed
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — assessment and initial teaching require nursing judgment and are never delegated to a UAP.

opts.text: Delegate the deteriorating resident's vital-sign monitoring and interpretation to the CNA and check back at end of shift
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the unstable resident requires RN assessment and ongoing judgment, not end-of-shift follow-up.

opts.text: Direct the LVN to push and titrate an IV medication on the acutely unstable resident
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — IV push/titration and unstable-resident management are outside typical LVN scope and are RN responsibilities.

opts.text: Dismiss the CNA's report of decreased intake and coughing because the resident 'looks okay'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — UAP-reported abnormal data must be assessed and acted upon, not dismissed.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Go to and assess the deteriorating resident (airway, breathing, oxygenation, mental status, vital signs) using clinical judgment
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The unstable resident takes priority and requires RN assessment.

opts.text: Hold PO, position upright, provide oxygen, notify the provider, and arrange a swallow evaluation/workup
opts.rat: #2 — Protect the airway and start the diagnostic workup for the new fever and confusion.

opts.text: Delegate clear, specific tasks to the LVN (scheduled meds, reinforce teaching, established dressing) and the CNAs (ADLs, routine vitals, I&O, glucose), stating what and when to report
opts.rat: #3 — Distribute routine work with the right direction so the rest of the unit is covered.

opts.text: Complete the new admission's RN assessment, medication reconciliation, and initial teaching
opts.rat: #4 — Fulfill the RN-only responsibilities for the incoming resident.

opts.text: Supervise, remain available, follow up on delegated tasks, and evaluate outcomes; re-prioritize as conditions change
opts.rat: #5 — Supervision and evaluation close the loop and remain the RN's accountability.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Classify each action as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE delegation/supervision.

opts.text: The RN assessed Mr. Ellison herself rather than delegating the unstable resident's assessment
opts.rat: Assessment and care of an unstable resident are RN responsibilities.

opts.text: The CNA's report of low intake and coughing prompted the RN to hold PO and arrange a swallow evaluation
opts.rat: Acting on UAP-reported data to protect the airway is correct nursing judgment.

opts.text: Stable residents' ADLs, routine vitals, and glucose checks were delegated to the CNAs with clear direction and a report-back plan
opts.rat: Routine tasks for stable residents delegated with the Five Rights satisfied.

opts.text: The LVN administered stable residents' scheduled medications and reinforced established teaching under RN supervision
opts.rat: Within LVN scope for stable residents under the RN's direction.

opts.text: The RN delegated the new admission's full assessment and initial teaching to a CNA to save time
opts.rat: ⚠️ Assessment and initial teaching require nursing judgment and are never delegated to a UAP.

opts.text: The RN told the CNA to monitor the deteriorating resident's vitals and report only at end of shift, with no RN assessment
opts.rat: ⚠️ The unstable resident needs prompt RN assessment and judgment, not deferred UAP monitoring.

opts.text: The LVN was directed to push and titrate an IV medication on the acutely unstable resident
opts.rat: ⚠️ IV push/titration and unstable-resident care are RN responsibilities outside typical LVN scope.

opts.text: After delegating tasks, the RN followed up, verified completion, and evaluated outcomes, knowing accountability for the delegation remained with her
opts.rat: Supervision and evaluation complete the delegation process; accountability stays with the RN.

opts.text: The CNA's report of a new abnormal finding was dismissed because the resident 'looked fine'
opts.rat: ⚠️ Abnormal UAP-reported data must be assessed and acted upon, not dismissed.
